content.text: The Case for a Layered Enforcement Architecture

An Acceptable Use Policy without enforcement is a compliance document that employees sign once and forget. Effective AUP enforcement operates across four mutually reinforcing layers: preventive controls that stop prohibited actions before they happen, detective controls that surface violations that slip through, corrective controls that respond proportionately when violations are confirmed, and adaptive controls that update the system based on what is learned from incidents.

No single layer is sufficient. Preventive controls can be circumvented by determined actors. Detective controls generate false positives that erode credibility if not triaged carefully. Corrective responses that are either too harsh or too lenient both undermine the policy. Adaptive learning is the layer most organisations skip — and the one that determines whether the policy keeps pace with how GenAI use actually evolves inside the organisation.
The goal of AUP enforcement is not to catch every violation; it is to make compliant behaviour the path of least resistance and to respond quickly when the controls fail.
Enforcement Architecture Overview

attrs.alt: Four-layer AUP enforcement architecture: Preventive, Detective, Corrective, and Adaptive Learning

content.text: The four-layer model maps directly to the control taxonomy used in information security, adapted for the specifics of GenAI use:
Preventive — technical and organisational controls that block or gate prohibited actions upstream.
Detective — monitoring and logging controls that identify violations after they occur.
Corrective — response workflows that limit damage and impose proportionate consequences.
Adaptive — feedback loops that use violation data to improve preventive and detective controls over time.
Preventive Controls
Approved Tool Registry and Access Gating

The most effective preventive control is ensuring employees can only reach approved GenAI tools in the first place. This requires an enterprise-managed tool registry — a list of approved vendors and endpoints — backed by network-level enforcement:
Web proxy category policies that block known unapproved AI service domains.
Endpoint management that restricts browser extensions to an approved list, preventing users from installing unofficial AI plugins.
Single sign-on (SSO) integration so that access to approved GenAI tools is governed by the same identity and access management (IAM) platform as other enterprise systems.
Role-Based Access Control for Conditional Uses

Conditional use cases — HR analysis, legal drafting, customer content generation — require explicit role-based access controls so that not every employee can invoke high-risk capabilities. In practice this means:
The GenAI platform's API key or enterprise SSO group restricts access to HR-specific analysis features to the 'hr_approved' IAM group.
Finance GenAI features that can interact with payment systems are gated behind a 'fintech_genai' role assigned only after manager approval and training completion.
Audit trail annotations record which role authorisation was used when a conditional-use request is made.
Inline Prompt Governance

For organisations that route GenAI calls through an internal middleware or API gateway, prompt governance can be enforced programmatically before the call reaches the model. The governance layer classifies the prompt, checks it against AUP rules, and either permits, flags, or blocks the request in real time.
Detective Controls
Centralised Prompt and Response Logging

Approved enterprise GenAI platforms must be configured to emit structured audit logs for every request. These logs are forwarded to the organisation's SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) system and retained for a minimum of 90 days for general use and 12 months for regulated use cases (financial services, healthcare).
Log Field
Purpose
Retention Sensitivity
user_id / employee_id
Identify the initiating employee
Medium — access-controlled
timestamp_utc
Sequence events; match to other audit trails
Low
tool_name / endpoint
Confirm use was via approved platform
Low
data_classification_declared
Cross-check against tool permissions
Low
prompt_hash (SHA-256)
Non-reversible fingerprint for dedup; not full text
Low
response_length_tokens
Usage analytics; detect unusually large outputs
Low
governance_decision
permit / flag_and_permit / block
Medium
role_authorisation_used
Which RBAC role authorised conditional access
Medium

Storing full prompt text is generally inadvisable: prompts can contain sensitive data, and full storage creates a secondary data protection risk. The preferred approach is to store a SHA-256 hash of the prompt for deduplication and anomaly detection, while retaining full text only for flagged or blocked requests where the content is itself the evidence.
Shadow AI Detection via Proxy Analytics

The proxy log is the most reliable source for detecting unapproved tool use because it captures the destination domain regardless of whether the user is using a browser, a desktop application, or an API client. Shadow AI detection analyses:
Usage Analytics and Anomaly Detection
Beyond shadow AI, usage analytics on approved platforms surface behavioural anomalies that warrant investigation. Meaningful signals include:
A user whose daily prompt volume spikes by more than 5× their 30-day average — could indicate a bulk data extraction attempt.
A user submitting prompts that consistently fail the governance filter, suggesting deliberate probing for gaps.
A single session that downloads an unusually large volume of text from a GenAI tool — possible output harvesting.
A cluster of employees from the same team all adopting a new unapproved tool in the same week — possible peer-driven shadow AI spread.
Corrective Controls — Violation Response Workflow
Triage and Severity Classification

When a detective control surfaces a potential violation, the first step is triage: confirm the event is genuine, classify its severity, and route it to the appropriate response workflow. A low-severity event that triggers a full HR investigation burns credibility and makes employees feel surveilled; a high-severity event handled informally lets serious misconduct go unchecked.
Access Revocation and Containment

For HIGH and CRITICAL violations, access to all GenAI tools must be revoked immediately while investigation proceeds. Access revocation is not a disciplinary outcome — it is a containment measure to stop further potential harm. Revocation is implemented via the IAM platform by removing the employee from the GenAI access group, which propagates to all SSO-integrated tools within minutes. The employee is informed in writing that access has been suspended pending investigation, with an estimated timeline for review.
Incident Record and Reporting

Every confirmed violation generates an incident record that feeds into the quarterly governance report. The record captures the detection source, severity, response taken, and outcome. Aggregate reporting (never individual names in board-level reports) covers:
Total violations by severity and category over the quarter.
Trend comparison to the previous quarter — rising shadow AI events or PII incidents are early warning signals.
Mean time to detect and mean time to respond, measured against SLA targets.
Control effectiveness: what percentage of violations were caught by preventive controls vs. detective controls.
Adaptive Controls — Learning and Improvement
Adaptive controls close the feedback loop between incidents and prevention. Three mechanisms drive continuous improvement:
Mechanism
Input
Output
Quarterly incident review
Violation log; severity distribution; detection source breakdown
AUP revision items; control gap backlog; updated prohibited-use examples
Post-violation re-training
Specific violation type (e.g. shadow AI, PII in prompt)
Targeted e-learning module; manager debrief; re-acknowledgment of AUP
Industry threat intelligence
Peer-org incident reports; regulator guidance; vendor disclosures
New prohibited categories; updated tool risk ratings; proxy blocklist additions

The adaptive layer also feeds back into the preventive layer. If the quarterly review shows that 40% of violations involve a specific unapproved tool that employees keep reaching for, the appropriate response may be to evaluate that tool for enterprise approval rather than continuing to block it — the AUP serves the organisation's needs, not the reverse.
Summary

AUP enforcement is most effective when it is layered: preventive controls reduce the volume of violations by making compliant behaviour the easiest path; detective controls catch what slips through; corrective controls respond proportionately; and adaptive controls use incident data to continuously improve the system.
Preventive controls include an approved tool registry with network-level enforcement, role-based access for conditional uses, and inline prompt governance for API-mediated access.
Detective controls rely on centralised SIEM-forwarded audit logs, shadow AI detection via proxy analytics, and usage anomaly monitoring on approved platforms.
Corrective workflows triage violations by severity, with SLA targets ranging from 1 hour (CRITICAL) to 72 hours (LOW) and proportionate responses from reminder to immediate suspension.
Adaptive controls feed violation data into quarterly AUP reviews, targeted re-training, and control-gap remediation items.
Log full prompt text only for flagged or blocked requests — hash-only logging for permitted requests limits secondary data protection risk.
Shadow AI is the most common enforcement challenge; proxy-based detection is more reliable than endpoint-based detection because it is harder for employees to circumvent.
